Parts Display Redesign

All MPNs treated equally — no primary vs substitute distinction. Pick the layout that works best.

Option A — Inline Chips All MPNs as equal chips in one cell, single row per requirement
★ Recommended. Compact, scannable, and visually reinforces that all MPNs are equal. Works well for 1-4 MPNs per requirement.
Most compact — no extra rows or vertical space
Rows with 5+ MPNs will wrap and get taller
Instantly clear which MPNs belong to which requirement
MPN column becomes wider than other columns
All MPNs visually identical — no hierarchy
Less room for per-MPN metadata (sightings count per MPN)
RFQ-2026-0047 — Acme Electronics 5 parts
Part Numbers Brand Qty Target Status Sightings

LM7805CT
Texas Instruments 100 $0.3200 Open 8
ESP32-WROVER-E ESP32-WROVER-B
Espressif 50 — Open 0
ADS1115IDGST
Texas Instruments 200 $1.8000 Quoted 23
IRFZ44NPBF IRF3205PBF IRLZ44NPBF STP55NF06L
Infineon / ST 500 $0.1500 Sourcing 45
Option B — Stacked Lines Primary MPN as row text, other chips wrap below — two visual lines per requirement
Primary MPN stands out as readable text
Creates visual hierarchy — contradicts "all equal" goal
Familiar table feel — one main value per row
Two-line rows feel inconsistent with single-MPN rows

Option C — Grouped Rows Each MPN gets its own row, visually grouped under the same requirement
Each MPN has its own row — room for per-MPN sightings
Takes 3x vertical space for a 3-MPN requirement
Clean, traditional table layout
Harder to scan — which rows are one requirement?
Could show per-MPN search results separately
Most complex to implement — grouping logic needed
